what_it_does: This file renders human-readable operational views of a service registry: a grouped status board with per-service state, port, GPU VRAM budget, and notes; and a health checklist that checks either a remote health URL for hosted services or a local port for everything else.
observations: Imports from sibling modules registry, systemd, and gpu rather than the stdlib
status() builds a multi-line formatted report with ANSI bold group headers
It dynamically sizes the service-key column to the longest key
GPU services get a VRAM budget line computed via budget_vram(); non-GPU services show no VRAM
Hosted services are handled specially in health() using a health_url and _ping_url()
Local services in health() are checked with port_open() on their configured port
Services without a port and not hosted are silently skipped by health()
standards: Tightly couples presentation logic with operational semantics (verdict, port/URL checks, GPU budgeting)
Uses ANSI escape sequences directly in the status output
Differentiates remote-hosted vs local-process health checks in one function
conventions: Module docstring states the file's scope and claims stdlib-only
Uses f-strings and list-join for output assembly
Names functions after the view they produce (status, health)
Imports a private-looking helper _ping_url from systemd
concerns: _ping_url() is imported with a leading underscore but used as a dependency, suggesting a boundary mismatch
ANSI formatting may be undesirable for non-terminal consumers or logs
health() silently ignores services that have no port and are not hosted, which could hide misconfigured entries
No fallback for missing expected keys (e.g., v['manage']['type'], v['group'])
notes: A CLI formatter that turns a structured service registry into a readable status dashboard and a liveness checklist.
questions: What exact shape does the reg dictionary have (services, groups, manage, port, title, note)?
What tuple does verdict() return (label, sym)?
What does read_gpu() return and what does it mean when it is falsy?
Is _ping_url() intended to be a public API of systemd?
What are the units and semantics of committed_mb(), ceiling_mb(), and budget_vram()?
purpose_vs_actual: The stated purpose is human/AI-readable status and health views; the file matches that purpose.
intention_signals: Provide a single-file, at-a-glance command-center view of services that combines liveness, grouping, ports, and GPU memory budgeting so operators can quickly see what is running and how much VRAM is committed.
novelty: It merges GPU VRAM budgeting directly into the same status board as service liveness and group headings, and treats hosted off-box services as first-class citizens with URL-based health checks.
contribution: This file contributes the presentation layer for operational status and health: the layout, formatting, grouping, annotation, and health-symbol logic that a fused system would keep for rendering registry state to a terminal or log.
summary_for_embedding: A CLI renderer that formats a service registry into a grouped, annotated status board and a per-service health checklist.
intention_for_embedding: To give operators and scripts a readable, single-file view of service status and liveness.

symbols.does: Builds and returns a formatted, grouped status report for all services in the registry, including symbols, ports, GPU VRAM budgets, and notes.
symbols.description: status(reg) iterates over service groups, collects members, and emits a human-readable board. For each service it asks verdict() for a state label and symbol, prints the port and title, and adds an approximate GPU VRAM budget for GPU-backed services. It ends with a GPU budget summary line comparing committed memory to the registry ceiling and measured GPU usage.

symbols.does: Builds and returns a per-service health checklist using either remote URL pings for hosted services or local port checks for port-bearing services.
symbols.description: health(reg) loops over every service. If the service is hosted, it uses a configured health_url and _ping_url() to mark it alive or dead. Otherwise, if the service has a port, it uses port_open() to decide liveness. Services that are neither hosted nor port-bearing are omitted from the output.
